heading: The Core Problem: Android Sandbox Architecture
content: Android's security model isolates every app inside a sandbox to prevent malicious apps from accessing system resources or interfering with other apps. Termux, despite being a powerful Linux environment emulator, runs within these same constraints -- any process inside Termux, including AI models, is trapped within the sandbox boundary. Consequences: the AI cannot read or interact with the device's display (screen access), cannot launch, close, or manipulate other apps (app control), cannot toggle WiFi, Bluetooth, or modify system preferences (system settings), and has limited access to sensors, cameras, and other hardware components (hardware control). True AI-driven device automation requires breaking out of this sandbox while maintaining system stability and security.

heading: Breaking the Sandbox: Shizuku and Rish
content: Shizuku (privilege broker) is an app that uses Android's Wireless Debugging feature to acquire ADB permissions without root access. Rish (command wrapper) is a special shell command exported from Shizuku into Termux that executes commands with elevated system privileges. Once the necessary files are exported from Shizuku into Termux, the rish command becomes available, and any command wrapped in rish executes with system-level privileges, for example rish -c "input keyevent 3" to force home screen navigation, rish -c "monkey -p com.google.android.youtube 1" to launch YouTube, or rish -c "svc wifi disable" to toggle WiFi state. This is the critical bridge out of Termux: the AI, running in the sandbox, can execute commands that directly manipulate the Android system through the rish tunnel.

heading: The Brain: llama.cpp Inference Engine
content: Python frameworks add interpreted-execution overhead and higher memory use; cloud APIs require internet, add latency, and raise privacy concerns; generic binaries are suboptimal for the target CPU architecture. The solution is to automatically download and compile llama.cpp, a pure C/C++ inference engine, using cmake to target the device's specific processor architecture for near-optimal execution speed, configured to run as a persistent server exposing an OpenAI-compatible API on localhost:8080 so any component in the stack can talk to the AI over standard HTTP. Server startup: ./server -m model.gguf --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8080 -c 4096 --timeout 300.

heading: The Logic Glue: Node.js and Telegram Bridge
content: A lightweight JavaScript bridge (telegram_bot.js, Node.js) maps user chat messages to Shizuku/rish commands, acting as the orchestration layer between natural-language input and system-level execution. Command processing flow: User Input to Telegram Bot to Few-Shot Prompting to LLM Inference to Command Parsing to Rish Execution to System Action. Steps: (1) Telegram bot receives a natural-language command like Open YouTube; (2) Node.js constructs a prompt with few-shot examples mapping intents to ADB commands; (3) local AI processes the prompt and returns a structured response; (4) the response is parsed for a CMD: prefix and the raw command extracted; (5) the command is wrapped in the rish tunnel and executed with system privileges. Example: AI responds CMD: monkey -p com.google.android.youtube 1, Node.js does const command = aiResponse.replace("CMD:", "").trim(); const rishCommand = `rish -c "${command}"`; exec(rishCommand); and YouTube launches on device using only local resources, no internet required. Note on code safety: this exec(rishCommand) pattern interpolates a raw command string from LLM output directly into a shell exec call, which is a command-injection-shaped pattern if the LLM output were ever adversarially influenced (e.g. via prompt injection from screen content in the vision pipeline) -- worth flagging since this class of risk is exactly what OmniRoute's own CLAUDE.md hard rules warn against (never string-interpolate untrusted values into shell exec calls).

heading: Vision Integration: OpenClaw and Multimodal AI
content: Qwen, the vision model used here, is multimodal -- it processes and understands visual information alongside text, letting the AI see the screen and decide based on visual context. OpenClaw is integrated as a third-party automation framework providing rapid screenshot capture (high-frequency, for real-time visual analysis), UI layout analysis (extraction of interactive elements and coordinates), and element detection (buttons, text fields, other UI components). OpenClaw is designed to connect to ChatGPT over the internet, conflicting with the offline-first requirement, so a custom executable openclaw-local intercepts OpenClaw's environment paths and redirects all API calls to the local llama.cpp server (BASE_URL http://127.0.0.1:8080/v1, a dummy API_KEY for compatibility, with /v1/chat/completions, /v1/models, /v1/embeddings all redirected). Vision-to-action pipeline: Take Screenshot to UI Analysis to Send to LLM to Vision Inference to Extract Coordinates to Rish Tap Command -- a closed-loop system where OpenClaw captures the screen, feeds visual data entirely offline into the local llama.cpp engine, determines target UI element locations, and uses rish to tap them natively.

heading: System Architecture Overview
content: Layered stack: User Interface layer is the Telegram Bot, providing natural-language input/output; Orchestration layer includes the Node.js Bridge (prompt engineering, command parsing, execution flow) and OpenClaw Local (visual capture, UI analysis, coordinate extraction); AI Inference layer is llama.cpp plus Qwen (local LLM inference, multimodal understanding); Privilege layer is Shizuku plus Rish (ADB privilege acquisition, command execution); System layer is the Android APIs (hardware control, app management, settings).

heading: Executive Summary
content: Summary quote: a lightweight C++ AI inference server is deployed directly on the mobile device, communicating through a Telegram bot interface running in the background; by granting the system Shizuku (Developer) privileges, the AI breaks out of the command terminal sandbox and natively controls phone settings, applications, and hardware, all while completely disconnected from the internet. Claimed key achievements: 100 percent offline operation (no data leaves the device, all inference local); native C++ optimization (architecture-specific compilation for maximum inference speed); no root required (leverages official Android debugging features only); vision-capable multimodal AI (sees, understands, and interacts with on-screen elements). This is the project's own technical marketing document; performance/capability claims are as stated by the authors, not independently benchmarked.

heading: Relevance to This Project
content: This is one of the strongest available proof-of-concept precedents for the entire Omni Claw/Novus Agenti vision, demonstrating with a named working architecture several mechanisms Omni Claw's blueprint describes only as design intent. Sandbox-breaking via privilege escalation (Shizuku/Rish, no root) is a concrete answer to how Omni Claw's Kotlin app could gain System Actions-class capabilities described in its Agentic Capabilities Map, via an alternate mechanism (ADB-based privilege broker) alongside Accessibility Service. A local llama.cpp OpenAI-compatible server on 127.0.0.1:8080 is the same core pattern as Omni Claw's C++ daemon ort_server on 127.0.0.1:8080, and the same pattern used throughout the llm-wiki-termux-setup skill package -- convergent validation this is the standard shape for on-device local inference serving. OpenClaw's vision-to-action pipeline (screenshot to UI analysis to LLM to coordinate extraction to tap) is a real working version of Omni Claw's Triple-Mode Screen Vision Framework Mode 2 (Contextual Point-and-Shoot), and is also the same OpenClaw project named in the Final Memory Layer/P2P doc as one of the repos the user is weaving together (there recalled as Mimir Claw/OpenClaw, the dual-agent session persistence engine) -- this document is likely the clearest existing description of what that OpenClaw integration actually does.
